name: Classic Country Sourdough (Tartine-Style)
description: A 75% hydration country loaf with a crackly crust, open crumb, and mild tang — the foundational sourdough recipe adapted from Chad Robertson's Tartine Bakery method.

ingredients.item: active sourdough starter (100% hydration, at peak)

steps.title: Levain / Check Starter
steps.description: Confirm your starter is at peak activity: it should have doubled, smell yeasty-sour, and pass the float test (drop a small spoonful in water — it should float). If using a separate levain, mix 50g mature starter + 100g bread flour + 100g water (80°F/27°C) and let ripen 4-6 hours at 75-78°F/24-26°C until doubled and domed.
steps.visualCue: Levain is domed or just starting to fall, full of bubbles, smells tangy and yeasty.

steps.title: Autolyse
steps.description: In a large bowl combine 900g bread flour + 100g whole wheat flour. Add 700g of the warm water (reserve 50g). Mix with your hand until no dry flour remains. Cover and rest.
steps.visualCue: Shaggy, rough dough with no dry streaks. No gluten development yet — that's fine.

steps.title: Add Levain
steps.description: Add 200g ripe levain on top of the autolysed dough. Using wet hands, squeeze and fold the levain into the dough until fully incorporated. The dough will feel slippery at first.
steps.visualCue: No white streaks of levain remain; dough is uniform.

steps.title: Add Salt
steps.description: Dissolve 20g salt in the reserved 50g warm water, then pour over the dough and incorporate fully with more squeezing and folding.
steps.visualCue: Dough will initially resist, then come back together smoothly.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation with Stretch-and-Folds
steps.description: Bulk ferment at 76-78°F/24-26°C for 3.5-5 hours total. During the first 2 hours, perform 4 sets of stretch-and-folds 30 minutes apart: grab one side of the dough, stretch it up high, fold it to the opposite side. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat until you've completed all 4 sides (= 1 set). Cover between sets.
steps.visualCue: By the end of bulk, dough should be 50-75% larger, domed on top, jiggly like Jello when you shake the bowl, full of bubbles on the sides and bottom, and pulling away from the bowl sides cleanly.

steps.title: Pre-shape
steps.description: Gently turn the dough out onto an unfloured surface. For 2 loaves, divide into two equal pieces with a bench scraper. Shape each loosely into a round by folding the edges inward, then flip seam-side down and drag toward you across the bench to build surface tension. Rest uncovered.
steps.visualCue: Rounds hold their shape, have a smooth taut skin, and aren't spreading flat.

steps.title: Final Shape
steps.description: Flour your work surface and the top of each round. Flip the round (floured side down). For a batard: fold bottom third up, fold sides in, then roll from bottom to top, applying tension. For a boule: fold edges to center, flip, and drag in circular motions. Place seam-side UP in a well-floured (rice flour works best) proofing basket (banneton).
steps.visualCue: Shaped loaf is tight, holds a round/oval form, and has a smooth unbroken surface.

steps.title: Cold Retard (Overnight Proof)
steps.description: Cover bannetons with a shower cap or plastic wrap and refrigerate uncovered for 8-16 hours (overnight). Cold fermentation slows the yeast, deepens flavor, and makes scoring easier.
steps.visualCue: Dough will feel firm and cold when you take it out. It should not have dramatically overflowed the basket.

steps.title: Preheat
steps.description: Place a Dutch oven (with lid) in the oven. Preheat to 500°F/260°C for at least 45-60 minutes. A thoroughly preheated vessel is critical for oven spring.
steps.visualCue: The Dutch oven should feel searingly hot; its color may darken slightly.

steps.title: Score and Bake (Covered)
steps.description: Flip the cold loaf out of the banneton onto a piece of parchment. Working quickly, score the top with a lame or sharp razor at a 30-45° angle — one long curved slash or an ear cut. Lift the parchment and lower into the screaming-hot Dutch oven. Put the lid on immediately.
steps.visualCue: The ear should begin opening within 10 minutes; steam visible when you crack the lid.

steps.title: Bake (Uncovered)
steps.description: Remove the lid and reduce oven to 450°F/232°C. Continue baking until the crust is a deep mahogany brown and the internal temperature reads 205-210°F (96-99°C) on an instant-read thermometer.
steps.visualCue: Deep brown crust with no pale spots. Knocking the bottom should sound hollow. Internal temp 205-210°F/96-99°C.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Transfer to a wire rack and cool for at least 1 hour before cutting. The crumb continues to set as steam escapes — cutting too early gives a gummy crumb.
steps.visualCue: Crust crackles and sings as it cools. Loaf sounds hollow when tapped.

tips: Rice flour in the banneton prevents sticking far better than all-purpose flour.
Dough temperature matters: aim for a final dough temp of 76-78°F/24-26°C after mixing. Adjust water temp to hit this target.
If your dough is spreading flat on the bench, it may be overproofed or understrengthened — tighten your folds or shorten bulk.
Score at a shallow 30-45° angle (nearly parallel to the loaf surface) for an ear; a 90° straight-down cut gives a more rustic split.
If the crust burns before the crumb is done, tent with foil in the last 10 minutes.

name: Beginner Sourdough Loaf
description: A forgiving, lower-hydration (68%) sourdough loaf with a straightforward same-day or overnight schedule — designed for first-time sourdough bakers.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: In a large bowl, combine flour, water, and starter. Mix with your hand or a stiff spatula until no dry flour remains. The dough will look rough — that's fine. Cover and rest.
steps.visualCue: Shaggy, sticky dough with no dry pockets.

steps.title: Add Salt
steps.description: Sprinkle salt over dough, dimple it in with wet fingertips, and mix thoroughly for 2-3 minutes until incorporated.
steps.visualCue: Salt is fully dissolved into the dough; no crunchy spots.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation with Coil Folds
steps.description: Over the next 4-5 hours at room temperature, perform 4 coil folds spaced 30 minutes apart. For a coil fold: wet your hands, slide them under the dough, lift the center and let the ends fall and tuck under. Rotate 90° and repeat. After 4 sets, leave the dough undisturbed for the rest of bulk.
steps.visualCue: After bulk: dough is 50-75% larger, has a domed surface, jiggles like Jello when shaken, and bubbles are visible through the bowl sides.

steps.title: Shape
steps.description: Gently turn dough onto an unfloured surface. Fold the top down, the bottom up, and the sides in to form a rough rectangle. Then roll from bottom to top, pulling toward you slightly to build tension. Alternatively, do a simple envelope fold and flip seam-side down.
steps.visualCue: Loaf holds its shape without spreading, has a smooth skin.

steps.title: Proof
steps.description: Place seam-side up in a well-floured 8-inch proofing basket or a bowl lined with a floured kitchen towel. Cover. Option A — same day: proof at room temp 1-2 hours until puffy but not overflowed. Option B — overnight: refrigerate 8-12 hours.
steps.visualCue: Dough should puff up noticeably. Poke test: a floured finger poke springs back slowly and only halfway — if it springs back instantly it needs more time; if it doesn't spring back at all, it's overproofed.

steps.title: Preheat and Score
steps.description: Preheat oven with a Dutch oven inside to 475°F/245°C for 45 minutes. Flip the loaf onto parchment, score with a sharp knife or lame in one confident slash.
steps.visualCue: Score opens slightly as you cut — good sign of proper proof.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Bake covered 20 minutes at 475°F/245°C. Remove lid, reduce to 450°F/232°C, and bake 18-22 more minutes until deep golden-brown and internal temp reaches 205°F/96°C.
steps.visualCue: Golden-brown crust. Hollow thump when bottom is tapped. 205°F/96°C internal temp.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on a wire rack for at least 45 minutes before slicing.
steps.visualCue: Crust crackles as it cools. Slice reveals open-ish crumb with no gumminess.

tips: Lower hydration (68%) means the dough is stiffer and easier to handle — more forgiving than 75%+ recipes.
Your starter must be active and at or near peak. If it doesn't double reliably, give it 2-3 more feedings before baking.
Don't skip cooling — a gummy crumb almost always means the loaf was cut too soon.
All-purpose flour works here; bread flour gives a slightly chewier result.

name: Whole Wheat Sourdough
description: A 40% whole wheat sourdough with a nutty, earthy flavor, tender crumb, and wholesome nutrition — slightly denser than a white loaf but deeply satisfying.

steps.title: Autolyse
steps.description: Mix 300g bread flour and 200g whole wheat flour with 380g warm water until fully combined. Whole wheat absorbs more water than white flour, so the dough may feel stiffer than expected. Cover and rest.
steps.visualCue: Rough dough, no dry flour, slightly stiffer than an all-white dough.

steps.title: Add Starter and Salt
steps.description: Add 100g ripe starter and squeeze into dough. Once incorporated, dissolve 10g salt in 20g water and add, squeezing in thoroughly.
steps.visualCue: Dough is smooth and homogeneous.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Bulk ferment 4-5 hours at 76°F/24°C. Whole wheat ferments faster than white flour due to the bran. Perform 4 sets of stretch-and-folds in the first 2 hours (every 30 min), then let rest undisturbed. Watch dough carefully — whole wheat over-ferments quickly.
steps.visualCue: Dough grows 50-60%, feels airy and webby when stretched, sides of bowl show bubbles. Do not let it grow more than 75% or overfermentation will cause a dense crumb.

steps.title: Pre-shape and Bench Rest
steps.description: Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface, shape gently into a round, and let bench rest seam-side down, uncovered.
steps.visualCue: Round relaxes slightly but holds general shape.

steps.title: Final Shape
steps.description: Shape into a tight boule or batard with decisive folds and tension. Place seam-side up in a floured banneton.
steps.visualCue: Tight, domed surface with no tears.

steps.title: Cold Retard
steps.description: Cover and refrigerate 10-16 hours overnight.
steps.visualCue: Dough feels cold and slightly firm; has grown 10-20% in the fridge.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat Dutch oven at 500°F/260°C for 45 min. Score the cold loaf and bake covered 20 min at 500°F/260°C. Remove lid, reduce to 450°F/232°C, bake 20-22 more minutes until deep brown. Internal temp: 207-210°F/97-99°C.
steps.visualCue: Darker crust than a white loaf is normal. Internal temp 207°F/97°C minimum.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on wire rack at least 1 hour.
steps.visualCue: Crust is firm, crumb is moist but not gummy when sliced.

tips: Whole wheat ferments faster than white flour — shorten bulk time or lower your fermentation temperature if you see rapid rise.
Adding 1-2 tbsp of vital wheat gluten per 100g whole wheat flour significantly improves structure and oven spring.
A longer autolyse (60 min) helps hydrate the bran and develop gluten with less kneading.
Whole wheat loaves have a naturally tighter crumb than white loaves — that's expected, not a failure.

name: Dark Rye Sourdough (90% Rye)
description: A dense, moist, intensely flavored Northern European-style rye bread made with 90% rye flour and a rye sourdough starter — slices thinly and keeps for a week.

ingredients.item: rye sourdough starter (100% rye, at peak — 12h ferment)
dark rye flour (whole grain rye), divided
molasses or honey (optional, for color and slight sweetness)

steps.title: Build Rye Levain
steps.description: Mix 50g mature rye starter + 100g dark rye flour + 100g warm water. Cover and ferment at 75°F/24°C for 8-12 hours until very bubbly, domed, and sour-smelling. This becomes the 200g rye starter for the recipe.
steps.visualCue: Levain is very active, bubbling throughout, dome beginning to collapse. Smells sharply sour.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: In a large bowl, combine 200g ripe rye starter, 350g dark rye flour (reserve 100g), 50g bread flour, 420g warm water, 12g salt, optional caraway and molasses. Mix vigorously with a stiff spatula or wooden spoon for 3-4 minutes. Rye dough is more of a thick batter than a kneadable dough — it won't form a smooth ball.
steps.visualCue: Thick, sticky paste that holds its shape slowly but can't be shaped by hand like wheat dough.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Cover bowl and ferment at 76°F/24°C for 3-4 hours. Rye ferments quickly. You're looking for a 20-30% volume increase and a bubbly surface — rye won't develop gluten or jiggle like wheat dough.
steps.visualCue: Surface shows bubbles, dough has risen 20-30%, smells pleasantly sour.

steps.title: Pan and Final Proof
steps.description: Generously grease a 9x5-inch loaf pan. Wet your hands (rye dough is extremely sticky) and transfer dough into the pan, smoothing the top with wet fingertips or a wet spatula. Dust the top with rye flour. Cover and proof at room temp.
steps.duration: 1.5-2 hours at room temp, or 8-10 hours in fridge
steps.visualCue: Dough rises to just above the pan rim and shows small cracks on the flour-dusted surface.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat oven to 450°F/232°C. No Dutch oven needed — bake in the loaf pan. Place pan in oven and bake 15 minutes at 450°F/232°C, then reduce to 375°F/190°C and bake 40-45 more minutes. Cover loosely with foil if top browns too fast. Internal temp should reach 205°F/96°C.
steps.visualCue: Deep brown, firm top crust; loaf pulls slightly from pan sides. 205°F/96°C internal temp.

steps.title: Cool Completely — Do Not Cut Early
steps.description: This is critical for rye: cool on a wire rack for at least 2 hours, then wrap in a kitchen towel and rest 12-24 hours before cutting. Rye starch continues to set long after baking. Cutting early produces a gummy, wet crumb.
steps.visualCue: Crumb looks clean and moist (not wet) when sliced. Slices cleanly with a serrated knife.

tips: Never skip the 12-24 hour rest — rye bread truly needs it to avoid a gummy crumb.
Rye dough cannot be kneaded or folded like wheat dough; its stickiness is normal.
Adding 5-10% bread flour (as in this recipe) adds just enough gluten structure to prevent complete collapse.
Rye bread molds more slowly than wheat bread and keeps wrapped at room temp for 5-7 days.
Traditional additions include fennel seeds, coriander, or orange zest.

name: High-Hydration Open-Crumb Sourdough (85%)
description: An 85% hydration sourdough with a wildly open, custardy crumb and thin, shattering crust — the showstopper loaf that requires strong technique and a very active starter.

ingredients.item: active sourdough starter (at peak, very active)

steps.title: Levain
steps.description: Build a stiff or medium-stiff levain: 20g mature starter + 80g bread flour + 50g water (60% hydration). Ferment 4-5 hours until tripled. A stiffer levain produces more acidity and better gluten for high-hydration dough.
steps.visualCue: Levain tripled, very bubbly interior visible when jar is held to light, slight dome.

steps.title: Autolyse
steps.description: Mix 450g bread flour + 50g whole wheat flour with 375g water (reserve 50g). Mix until no dry flour remains. Autolyse helps high-hydration dough develop gluten passively before any manipulation.
steps.visualCue: Rough dough. The windowpane test will already show some stretch after 60 min.

steps.title: Add Levain and Salt
steps.description: Add 100g ripe levain and fold in. Rest 20 minutes. Then dissolve 11g salt in reserved 50g water, add, and incorporate with pincer method (pinch dough between fingers repeatedly, then fold over).
steps.visualCue: Dough is smooth, elastic, and very extensible — it should stretch a foot or more without tearing.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation (Lamination + Coil Folds)
steps.description: Bulk ferment 4-5 hours at 78°F/26°C. First 30 min: perform one lamination — gently stretch dough on a wet counter into a large thin rectangle without tearing, then fold like a letter and fold again. Next 2 hours: 3-4 coil folds every 30-40 minutes. Final 1.5-2 hours: hands off.
steps.visualCue: By end of bulk: dough has grown 60-80%, is very airy and delicate, shows large bubbles, jiggles dramatically when shaken — almost too loose-looking. This is correct for open crumb.

steps.title: Gentle Pre-shape
steps.description: CRITICAL: Handle this dough very gently or you'll degas it. Flour your bench generously. Scrape dough out, fold the edges in gently, and flip. Do NOT drag aggressively. Rest uncovered.
steps.visualCue: Round holds shape loosely; bubbles should still be visible through the dough.

steps.title: Final Shape (Tension Without Degassing)
steps.description: Flour bench again. Flip the round, gently stretch and fold sides in, then roll upward with minimal pressure. The goal is surface tension without crushing the gas pockets. Transfer seam-side up to a heavily floured banneton lined with a linen couche if available.
steps.visualCue: Loaf is smooth, domed, and visibly still airy inside.

steps.title: Cold Retard
steps.description: Refrigerate uncovered (or loosely covered) for 10-14 hours. Cold proofing firms the dough for easier scoring and handling.
steps.visualCue: Dough is cold and firm. Surface should feel slightly tacky but not wet.

steps.title: Bake in Dutch Oven
steps.description: Preheat Dutch oven to 500°F/260°C for 60 minutes (longer preheat for high-hydration loaves). Score with confidence in one or two cuts. Bake covered 20 min at 500°F/260°C. Remove lid, reduce to 465°F/240°C, bake 20-25 more minutes. Internal temp 208-210°F/98-99°C.
steps.visualCue: Dramatic oven spring and ear development. Very open, irregular hole structure visible at cut. Thin, blistered, crackly crust.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on rack at least 90 minutes before cutting.
steps.visualCue: The crumb will be custardy and moist-looking — not gummy, just open and lacy.

tips: Use the highest-protein bread flour you can find (King Arthur Bread Flour at 12.7% is excellent).
Temperature control is everything at 85% hydration — keep dough at 76-78°F/24-26°C throughout bulk.
Lamination early in bulk builds enough gluten structure to hold all that gas.
Do not rush shaping or handle aggressively — every bubble you pop is crumb you lose.
If your loaf comes out gummy and flat, it is almost certainly bulk overfermentation. Try shorter bulk or cooler temp next time.

name: Sourdough Sandwich Loaf
description: A soft, sliceable sourdough sandwich bread with a tender crumb and mild tang, baked in a loaf pan — perfect for toast and sandwiches.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: Combine flour, water, starter, honey, and salt. Mix until a shaggy dough forms. Add softened butter and knead by hand 5-8 minutes (or use a stand mixer with dough hook on medium for 6 minutes) until the dough is smooth and passes the windowpane test.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, slightly tacky dough that doesn't stick to your hands. Stretches thin without tearing (windowpane).

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Place dough in a lightly oiled bowl. Cover and ferment at 75-77°F/24-25°C for 4-5 hours, performing 3 sets of stretch-and-folds in the first 90 minutes (30 min apart).
steps.visualCue: Dough grows 50-75%, feels pillowy and airy, surfaces shows a dome.

steps.title: Shape for Pan
steps.description: Turn dough onto an unfloured surface. Pat into a rectangle roughly as wide as your loaf pan. Roll up tightly from the short end (like a cinnamon roll without the filling). Pinch the seam closed.
steps.visualCue: Cylinder of dough fits snugly in the greased pan, seam side down.

steps.title: Pan Proof (Overnight or Same Day)
steps.description: Grease a 9x5-inch loaf pan. Place shaped dough in pan, cover with oiled plastic wrap. Option A — overnight: refrigerate 8-12 hours. Option B — same day: proof at room temp 2-3 hours.
steps.visualCue: Dough crowns 1/2 to 1 inch above the pan rim and jiggles gently when you move the pan.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat oven to 375°F/190°C (no Dutch oven needed). Remove plastic wrap. Optionally brush top with egg wash or melted butter for a shiny crust. Bake 35-40 minutes until deep golden brown on top and sides, and internal temp reaches 195-200°F/91-93°C. (Slightly lower than artisan loaves — enrichment keeps the crumb tender.)
steps.visualCue: Top is deep golden brown. Sides pull from pan. Internal temp 195-200°F/91-93°C. Hollow thump.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Remove from pan immediately and cool on a wire rack at least 45 minutes before slicing.
steps.visualCue: Soft, even crust. Slices cleanly with uniform crumb, no gummy spots.

tips: Adding butter or oil softens the crumb and produces that classic sandwich-bread texture.
Honey adds a hint of sweetness and feeds the yeast — sugar works too.
For a softer crust, brush with melted butter immediately out of the oven.
This recipe works great with up to 20% whole wheat flour added in place of some bread flour.

name: Cinnamon Raisin Sourdough
description: A lightly enriched sourdough loaf swirled with cinnamon sugar and plump raisins — fragrant, slightly sweet, and perfect toasted with butter.

steps.title: Soak Raisins
steps.description: Cover raisins in warm water and soak 15-20 minutes, then drain and pat dry. Pre-soaking prevents raisins from stealing moisture from the crumb.
steps.visualCue: Raisins are plump and soft, not shriveled.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: Combine flour, water, starter, honey, and salt. Mix until no dry flour remains. Add softened butter and work in. Cover and autolyse 30 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Smooth-ish dough with butter incorporated.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Bulk ferment 4-5 hours at 76°F/24°C with 4 sets of stretch-and-folds (every 30 min for first 2 hours). After the second fold set, add raisins: scatter over the dough and fold them in gently so they're evenly distributed without breaking.
steps.visualCue: Dough is risen 50-70%, airy, raisins distributed throughout. No pooling of raisin juice.

steps.title: Pre-shape and Bench Rest
steps.description: Gently pre-shape into a rough rectangle (for a pan loaf) or round (for a boule). Bench rest uncovered 20-30 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Dough relaxes to a flat oval.

steps.title: Fill and Shape
steps.description: Pat dough into a rectangle roughly the width of your pan. Mix cinnamon, sugar, and melted butter into a paste; spread evenly over the dough, leaving a 1-inch border on the far short edge. Roll tightly from the near short edge, pinch seam closed, tuck ends under.
steps.visualCue: Tight roll with cinnamon visible at the cut ends. Seam is sealed.

steps.title: Proof in Pan
steps.description: Place seam-side down in a greased 9x5-inch loaf pan. Cover and refrigerate overnight 8-12 hours, or proof at room temp 2-3 hours.
steps.visualCue: Loaf crowns about 1 inch above pan rim.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat oven to 375°F/190°C. Brush loaf with egg wash (1 egg + 1 tbsp water). Bake 35-40 minutes until deep golden-brown with an internal temperature of 195°F/91°C. Tent with foil after 20 minutes if top browns too quickly.
steps.visualCue: Golden-brown top, sides pulling from pan, 195°F/91°C internal temp.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Remove from pan immediately. Cool on wire rack 1 hour before slicing — the cinnamon filling needs to set.
steps.visualCue: Clean spiral of cinnamon when sliced. No gummy crumb.

tips: Always soak raisins first — dry raisins pull moisture from the crumb and create gummy patches.
A tighter roll creates a more defined spiral; be decisive when rolling.
Cinnamon inhibits yeast slightly at high concentrations — keep filling to a thin layer and don't mix it into the dough itself.
Excellent toasted with salted butter or cream cheese.

name: High-Fiber Seeded Sourdough
description: A nutty, hearty sourdough loaf loaded with sesame, sunflower, and poppy seeds inside the crumb and pressed into the crust — inspired by The Perfect Loaf's seeded recipe.

ingredients.item: mixed seeds for crust coating (sesame + sunflower)

steps.title: Toast and Soak Seeds
steps.description: Toast sesame seeds in a dry skillet 2-3 min until golden. Combine all interior seeds (sesame, sunflower, poppy, flax) in a bowl with 75g warm water. Soak 20-30 minutes until water is absorbed. This prevents seeds from stealing dough moisture and makes them soft in the crumb.
steps.visualCue: Seeds have absorbed all the water; mixture looks like a thick porridge.

steps.title: Autolyse
steps.description: Mix bread flour, whole wheat, rye, and water (excluding seed soaker water) until no dry flour remains. Cover and rest.
steps.visualCue: Rough dough, no dry flour.

steps.title: Add Starter, Salt, and Seeds
steps.description: Add starter and salt to autolysed dough, incorporate thoroughly. Then add the soaked seed mixture and fold in over 2-3 minutes until evenly distributed.
steps.visualCue: Seeds evenly distributed throughout the dough.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Bulk ferment 4-5 hours at 76°F/24°C with 4 sets of stretch-and-folds over the first 2 hours (every 30 min). Dough will feel heavier than a plain loaf due to the seeds.
steps.visualCue: Dough has grown 50-60%, feels airy despite the weight of seeds. Bubbles visible.

steps.title: Pre-shape and Bench Rest
steps.description: Turn out, pre-shape into a round, bench rest 20-25 minutes uncovered.
steps.visualCue: Round holds shape with visible seeds throughout.

steps.title: Seed Crust and Final Shape
steps.description: Spread crust seeds in a thin layer on a damp kitchen towel or a plate. Final shape the loaf into a tight boule or batard, then roll the shaped loaf gently in the seeds to coat the exterior. Place seam-side up in a floured banneton.
steps.visualCue: Loaf is evenly coated with seeds on the exterior.

steps.title: Cold Retard
steps.description: Cover and refrigerate 10-16 hours.
steps.visualCue: Seeds set into the surface; loaf holds shape firmly.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat Dutch oven to 500°F/260°C for 45 min. Score cold loaf. Bake covered 20 min at 500°F/260°C. Remove lid, reduce to 450°F/232°C, bake 20-22 more min. Internal temp 205-208°F/96-98°C.
steps.visualCue: Seeds toasted golden on crust, deep brown loaf. Internal temp 205°F/96°C.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on wire rack 1 hour before cutting.
steps.visualCue: Seeds firmly attached to crust, clean slice through crumb.

tips: Always soak seeds — dry seeds will steal moisture from the dough and create hard, crunchy pockets in the crumb.
Rye flour boosts fermentation speed; shorten bulk slightly in warm kitchens.
For a nut-free version, replace sunflower seeds with more sesame or pepitas.
The soaked seeds add weight — bulk rise percentage will be slightly lower than a plain loaf.

name: Jalapeño Cheddar Sourdough
description: A boldly flavored sourdough loaf studded with sharp cheddar cheese and roasted jalapeños — spicy, savory, and irresistible with soup or eggs.

ingredients.item: sharp cheddar cheese, cubed into 1/2-inch pieces (cold)
jalapeño peppers, roasted, seeded (or pickled jalapeños, drained)

steps.title: Roast Jalapeños
steps.description: If using fresh jalapeños: roast under broiler 8-10 min, turning once, until charred and soft. Place in a covered bowl 10 min to steam. Peel, remove seeds (keep seeds if you want more heat), and slice into strips or rings. Pat dry. Cold or pickled jalapeños can be used directly — drain well and pat dry.
steps.visualCue: Jalapeños are soft, slightly charred, and no longer raw-smelling.

steps.title: Autolyse
steps.description: Mix flour and water (add garlic powder here if using). Rest covered 45 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Rough dough, no dry flour.

steps.title: Add Starter and Salt
steps.description: Add starter and salt, incorporate thoroughly with pincer method or squeezing. Do NOT add cheese or jalapeños yet.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, cohesive dough.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation — Add Mix-ins During Folds
steps.description: Bulk ferment 4.5-5 hours at 76°F/24°C. During the SECOND fold set (60 min into bulk): scatter cheese cubes and jalapeño pieces over the dough and fold in gently. Adding mix-ins mid-bulk (not at the start) preserves gluten structure. Perform 2 more fold sets at 30-min intervals.
steps.visualCue: Cheese and jalapeños evenly distributed. Dough risen 50-70%, airy and jiggly.

steps.title: Pre-shape and Bench Rest
steps.description: Gently turn dough out, pre-shape into a round. Bench rest uncovered 25 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Round relaxes but holds form. Cheese pieces visible.

steps.title: Final Shape
steps.description: Shape into a tight boule or batard. Reserve a few cheese cubes and jalapeño rings — press them into the seam side (which will become the top after scoring) for a decorative top. Place seam-side up in floured banneton.
steps.visualCue: Cheese and jalapeños studded on exterior; tight surface tension.

steps.title: Cold Retard
steps.description: Cover and refrigerate 10-16 hours.
steps.visualCue: Loaf is cold, firm, holds shape well.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat Dutch oven to 500°F/260°C for 45 min. Score cold loaf with confidence. Bake covered 20 min at 500°F/260°C. Remove lid, reduce to 450°F/232°C, bake 20-22 more min. Internal temp 205°F/96°C. Note: cheese on the surface may brown — this is delicious, not burnt.
steps.visualCue: Deep brown crust with golden, lacy cheese crust on top. Internal temp 205°F/96°C.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on wire rack 1 hour. The cheese needs to re-set after melting.
steps.visualCue: Cheese pockets in crumb are set, not molten.

tips: Keep cheese cold until adding it — warm cheese melts into the dough during bulk instead of staying in pockets.
Pickled jalapeños add tang and are easier to work with; fresh roasted give more smokiness.
Score over areas without surface cheese to prevent drag on the lame.
Sharp cheddar gives better flavor than mild; pepper jack adds an extra layer of heat.

name: Herb and Olive Oil Sourdough
description: A fragrant, Mediterranean-inspired sourdough loaf enriched with extra-virgin olive oil and folded with rosemary, thyme, and garlic — soft crumb, crisp crust.

ingredients.item: fresh rosemary, finely chopped (or 1 tbsp dried)

steps.title: Bloom Herbs in Olive Oil
steps.description: Gently warm olive oil in a small pan over low heat. Add rosemary, thyme, and garlic. Cook 2-3 minutes until fragrant but not brown. Remove from heat and cool completely. This blooms the aromatics and infuses the oil.
steps.visualCue: Oil smells powerfully herby; garlic is softened and golden, not browned.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: Combine flour, water, starter, and salt. Mix until no dry flour remains. Add the cooled herb oil and work in thoroughly. The dough will feel slightly slack from the oil.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, slightly tacky dough with herbs distributed throughout.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Bulk ferment 4.5-5 hours at 76°F/24°C with 4 sets of stretch-and-folds in the first 2 hours (every 30 min). Olive oil slightly slows fermentation, so give this bulk a little extra time.
steps.visualCue: Dough risen 50-65%, jiggly, aromatic. Herbs visible in the dough.

steps.title: Shape
steps.description: Pre-shape into a round, bench rest 25 minutes, then final shape into boule or batard. The olive oil makes this dough extensible and smooth to shape.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, tight surface. Herbs slightly visible in the skin.

steps.title: Cold Retard
steps.description: Place seam-side up in floured banneton, cover, refrigerate 10-16 hours.
steps.visualCue: Firm, cold loaf ready to score.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat Dutch oven at 500°F/260°C for 45 min. Score cold loaf. Bake covered 20 min at 500°F/260°C. Remove lid, reduce to 450°F/232°C, bake 20-22 more min. Internal temp 205°F/96°C.
steps.visualCue: Deep golden-brown crust. Herbs on the surface may darken slightly. Internal temp 205°F/96°C.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on wire rack 1 hour. Olive oil in the crumb makes it slightly more tender and keeps it moist longer.
steps.visualCue: Moist, fragrant crumb. Herb pieces visible throughout.

tips: Blooming herbs in oil intensifies their flavor far more than adding them raw.
Let the herb oil cool completely before adding to dough — hot oil kills the starter.
Add lemon zest with the fresh herbs for brightness.
Pairs exceptionally well with burrata, olive tapenade, or as a base for bruschetta.

name: Sourdough Discard Pancakes
description: Fluffy, tangy pancakes made with sourdough discard — the best use for unfed starter, with a subtle sour complexity that plain pancakes lack.

ingredients.item: buttermilk (or whole milk + 1 tbsp lemon juice)

steps.title: Mix Wet Ingredients
steps.description: In a large bowl, whisk together sourdough discard, buttermilk, eggs, melted butter, sugar, and vanilla until smooth.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, pourable batter with a slightly bubbly surface from the discard.

steps.title: Combine Dry Ingredients
steps.description: In a separate bowl, whisk flour, baking soda, baking powder, and salt together.
steps.visualCue: Dry ingredients evenly combined.

steps.title: Mix Batter (Do Not Overmix)
steps.description: Pour dry ingredients into wet and fold gently with a spatula until just combined. Lumps are fine — overmixing develops gluten and produces tough pancakes. Let batter rest 5 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Lumpy, thick batter. Some dry streaks are OK. You'll see bubbles forming from the baking soda + acid reaction.

steps.title: Cook Pancakes
steps.description: Heat a non-stick skillet or griddle over medium heat. Melt a small pat of butter or brush with oil. Pour 1/4 cup batter per pancake. Cook until bubbles form on the surface and edges look set and matte (not wet/shiny).
steps.visualCue: Bubbles form and pop on the surface, edges go from shiny to matte — flip now. Underside should be golden brown.

steps.title: Flip and Finish
steps.description: Flip once and cook 1-2 more minutes until the second side is golden brown.
steps.visualCue: Both sides are uniformly golden brown. Pancakes are puffed in the center.

tips: The more sour your discard, the tangier the pancakes — very tangy discard pairs great with sweet maple syrup.
Do not overmix — stop folding the moment dry flour disappears.
Keep finished pancakes warm in a 200°F/93°C oven on a baking sheet.
For extra-fluffy pancakes, separate the eggs, fold in whipped egg whites at the end.
Discard can come straight from the fridge — no need to bring to room temp.

name: Sourdough Discard Waffles
description: Crispy-exterior, tender-interior waffles with a gentle sourdough tang — the overnight version produces the best flavor and texture.

steps.title: Overnight Sponge (Optional but Recommended)
steps.description: The night before: whisk together discard, milk, and flour in a large bowl. Cover with plastic wrap and leave at room temperature 8-12 hours. The mixture will bubble and smell tangy — this is the overnight ferment that builds flavor.
steps.visualCue: Bubbly, slightly risen sponge with distinct sour smell in the morning.

steps.title: Add Wet Ingredients
steps.description: Into the sponge (or mixed discard + flour + milk if skipping overnight), whisk in egg yolks, melted butter, sugar, and vanilla.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, thick batter.

steps.title: Add Leaveners
steps.description: Add baking soda and salt; stir to combine. The batter will puff slightly from the soda reacting with sourdough acid.
steps.visualCue: Batter lightens slightly and becomes slightly airy.

steps.title: Fold in Whipped Egg Whites
steps.description: In a clean bowl, beat egg whites to stiff peaks. Fold into batter in two additions — fold gently to preserve air.
steps.visualCue: Batter is light and airy with no white streaks remaining.

steps.title: Cook Waffles
steps.description: Preheat waffle iron to medium-high heat. Brush with melted butter or non-stick spray. Pour batter to fill about 2/3 of the iron. Close lid and cook until steam stops escaping and waffles are crispy golden.
steps.visualCue: Steam stops coming from the waffle iron (key indicator). Waffle is deep golden with crispy grid texture.

tips: Never open the waffle iron early — wait until steam stops, about 4-5 minutes.
The egg white fold is the difference between crispy and soggy waffles.
Cool finished waffles directly on a wire rack (not a plate) to stay crispy.
Skip the overnight sponge if time-pressed — same-day still produces good results.
Freeze leftover waffles and reheat in a toaster for weekday breakfasts.

name: Sourdough Discard Crackers
description: Thin, shatteringly crisp crackers made from sourdough discard, olive oil, and whatever seeds or herbs you have on hand — incredibly addictive and use up discard in minutes.

ingredients.item: sesame seeds, everything bagel seasoning, or dried rosemary (choose your topping)

steps.title: Mix Cracker Dough
steps.description: Preheat oven to 325°F/165°C. In a bowl, mix discard, olive oil, and fine salt until smooth and homogeneous. The batter should be thin and pourable, similar to crepe batter.
steps.visualCue: Glossy, pourable batter. No lumps.

steps.title: Spread Thin
steps.description: Line two rimmed baking sheets with parchment. Pour half the batter on each sheet. Using a large offset spatula or the back of a spoon, spread in as thin and even a layer as possible — aim for nearly see-through (about 1-2mm thick). Scatter toppings and press gently into the surface. Sprinkle with flaky salt.
steps.visualCue: Thin, nearly translucent layer with no thick or thin patches. Toppings pressed in.

steps.title: Score (Optional)
steps.description: Use a pizza wheel or bench scraper to score the batter into cracker shapes before baking. This makes it easier to break into uniform pieces after baking.
steps.visualCue: Clean score lines through the batter to the parchment.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Bake at 325°F/165°C for 25-35 minutes, rotating pans halfway through. Crackers are done when they are uniformly golden, rigid (not soft or bendable), and pulling slightly from the parchment. Edges bake faster — check frequently after 20 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Even golden color across the sheet, rigid and crisp when bent, edges may be slightly darker. Any soft, bendy areas need more time.

steps.title: Cool and Break
steps.description: Remove from oven and cool completely on the pan. Crackers continue crisping as they cool. Break along scored lines into irregular shards.
steps.visualCue: Crackers shatter cleanly when broken. No soft or rubbery spots.

tips: Thinner spread = crispier crackers. This is the #1 variable to control.
If crackers turn out soft, they're underbaked — return to the oven for 5-10 more minutes at 300°F/150°C.
Store in an airtight container at room temp for up to 5 days.
Discard straight from the fridge works perfectly here — no need to temper.
Variations: add black pepper and parmesan, or za'atar and olive oil, or just everything bagel seasoning.

name: Sourdough Discard Banana Bread
description: Moist, deeply flavored banana bread elevated by sourdough discard — the acidity tenderizes the crumb and adds a subtle complexity.

ingredients.item: very ripe bananas (about 300g, peeled — the blacker the better)

steps.title: Preheat and Prep Pan
steps.description: Preheat oven to 350°F/175°C. Grease and flour a 9x5-inch loaf pan, or line with parchment.
steps.visualCue: Pan is greased evenly, no bare spots.

steps.title: Mash Bananas
steps.description: In a large bowl, mash bananas with a fork until mostly smooth with just a few small chunks. Very ripe bananas mash easily and provide the most sweetness.
steps.visualCue: Smooth puree with a few small chunks. Should look like thick applesauce.

steps.title: Mix Wet Ingredients
steps.description: Whisk sourdough discard, both sugars, eggs, melted butter, sour cream, and vanilla into the mashed banana until combined.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, uniform wet mixture — pale yellow and slightly thick.

steps.title: Add Dry Ingredients
steps.description: Add flour, baking soda, baking powder, salt, and cinnamon to the wet bowl. Fold with a spatula until just combined — stop mixing the moment no dry flour remains. Fold in walnuts or chocolate chips if using.
steps.visualCue: Thick, lumpy batter. No dry flour visible but not overworked.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Pour batter into prepared pan. Optionally slice a banana half lengthwise and press it on top for presentation. Bake 55-65 minutes until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean (or with moist crumbs, not wet batter). Tent with foil at 40 minutes if top is browning too fast.
steps.visualCue: Deep golden-brown top with a crack down the center. Toothpick comes out clean.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool in pan 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack and cool 20 more minutes before slicing.
steps.visualCue: Loaf pulls from pan sides, crumb is moist but springs back when pressed.

tips: The riper the banana, the sweeter and more flavorful the bread. Black-spotted bananas are ideal.
Sour cream or yogurt adds tenderness and amplifies the sourdough tang.
Do not overmix after adding flour — overmixed banana bread is rubbery.
For a crackly top crust, sprinkle with turbinado sugar before baking.

name: Sourdough Discard Pizza Dough
description: A chewy, flavorful pizza dough made with sourdough discard and a small amount of commercial yeast — delivers sourdough complexity without the long wait.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: Combine all ingredients in a bowl. Mix until a shaggy dough forms, then turn out and knead by hand 8-10 minutes (or stand mixer with dough hook 6 minutes on medium) until smooth and elastic. The dough should be slightly tacky but not sticky.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, elastic dough that springs back when poked. Passes the windowpane test.

steps.title: First Rise
steps.description: Place dough in a lightly oiled bowl. Cover with plastic wrap. Rise at room temp until doubled — about 1-2 hours. For more flavor, place in the fridge overnight (8-24 hours) after a 30-minute room temp rest.
steps.visualCue: Dough doubles in size, feels puffy and light.

steps.title: Divide and Rest
steps.description: Punch down dough, divide into 2 equal balls. Place on an oiled surface, cover with a damp towel, and rest 20-30 minutes. This relaxes the gluten so the dough stretches easily.
steps.visualCue: Dough balls are slightly relaxed and flatten a bit when resting.

steps.title: Stretch and Top
steps.description: Preheat oven to 500°F/260°C (or as hot as it goes) with a pizza stone or inverted baking sheet inside for at least 45 minutes. Stretch each dough ball on a floured surface by pressing from the center outward with your fingers, then over your fists, until about 12 inches wide and very thin in the center. Transfer to a floured pizza peel or parchment. Add sauce and toppings.
steps.visualCue: Dough is nearly translucent in the thinnest spots at the center. Edges are thicker (the 'cornicione').

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Slide pizza onto preheated stone or baking sheet. Bake 8-12 minutes until crust is blistered and golden, cheese is bubbling and starting to char in spots, and bottom is crispy and lightly charred.
steps.visualCue: Cornicione (crust edge) is puffed and charred in spots. Bottom is crispy and golden — lift an edge to check.

tips: 00 flour produces a silkier, more extensible dough than bread flour — better for thin Neapolitan-style crust.
Cold-fermented dough (overnight) develops far more flavor than same-day.
Never use a rolling pin — it degasses the dough and produces a flat, dense crust.
A screaming-hot oven is non-negotiable for good pizza. Preheat stone/steel for at least 45 minutes.

name: Sourdough Discard Flatbread
description: Soft, pliable flatbreads cooked in a cast-iron skillet from sourdough discard — ready in under 30 minutes, no oven required.

ingredients.item: all-purpose flour, plus more for dusting

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: In a bowl, mix discard, flour, salt, garlic powder, olive oil, and baking powder (if using) until a soft dough forms. Knead briefly in the bowl 1-2 minutes until smooth. If too sticky, add flour a tablespoon at a time.
steps.visualCue: Soft, pliable dough that barely sticks to hands. Like Play-Doh consistency.

steps.title: Divide and Roll
steps.description: Divide dough into 6-8 equal pieces. On a floured surface, roll each piece out to about 8 inches in diameter and 2-3mm thin.
steps.visualCue: Thin, even round with slightly irregular edges.

steps.title: Cook in Dry Skillet
steps.description: Heat a cast-iron skillet or heavy pan over medium-high heat until very hot (no oil needed). Cook each flatbread 1-2 minutes per side until charred spots appear, the dough puffs in places, and it feels dry and cooked through.
steps.visualCue: Dark char spots appear on the surface, bread puffs up, the cooked side looks dry and matte.

steps.title: Keep Warm and Serve
steps.description: Stack cooked flatbreads on a plate under a clean kitchen towel to keep them soft and pliable. Brush with garlic butter or olive oil immediately if desired.
steps.visualCue: Flatbreads are soft and pliable when stacked together, slightly charred.

tips: Do not add oil to the pan — a dry cast-iron gives the best char without burning.
Rest the dough 10 minutes for even softer flatbread.
Use immediately — flatbreads stiffen as they cool; rewarm briefly in a dry pan.
Works as a wrap, pizza base, or served alongside soups and dips.

name: Sourdough Discard Blueberry Muffins
description: Tall, domed blueberry muffins with a tender crumb and subtle tang from sourdough discard — a wonderful way to use discard in an everyday bake.

steps.title: Preheat and Prep
steps.description: Preheat oven to 425°F/220°C. Line a 12-cup muffin tin with liners or grease well. High initial heat creates tall, domed tops.
steps.visualCue: Oven is fully preheated before batter goes in.

steps.title: Whisk Dry Ingredients
steps.description: In a large bowl whisk together flour, sugar, baking powder, baking soda, and salt.
steps.visualCue: Evenly combined dry mix.

steps.title: Mix Wet Ingredients
steps.description: In a separate bowl whisk eggs, milk, sour cream, oil, vanilla, and sourdough discard until smooth.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, slightly bubbly liquid from the discard.

steps.title: Combine and Fold in Berries
steps.description: Pour wet into dry and fold with a spatula until just combined (10-12 folds max). Toss blueberries with 1 tsp flour, then fold gently into batter. Do not overmix.
steps.visualCue: Thick, lumpy batter. Berries coated and distributed. Flour coating on berries prevents sinking.

steps.title: Fill and Top
steps.description: Fill muffin cups to the very top — even a slight mound. This is what creates the domed top. Sprinkle turbinado sugar over each.
steps.visualCue: Cups are filled flush to the rim or slightly over.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Bake at 425°F/220°C for 5 minutes, then (without opening the oven) reduce to 375°F/190°C and bake 14-16 more minutes until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean and tops are golden.
steps.visualCue: Tall domed tops, golden brown, toothpick clean. The blast of high heat is what causes the dome.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool in pan 5 minutes, then transfer to wire rack.
steps.visualCue: Muffins release cleanly from liners. Crumb is tender, not gummy.

tips: The high-heat start (425°F blast) is the secret to domed muffin tops — don't skip it.
Coating berries in flour prevents them from sinking to the bottom.
Do NOT overmix — 10-12 folds is enough. Lumpy batter = tender crumb.
Frozen blueberries work well; do not thaw before folding in.

name: Sourdough Soft Pretzels
description: Chewy, golden soft pretzels with a glossy baked crust and a hit of sourdough tang — made with discard and a small amount of instant yeast.

ingredients.item: coarse pretzel salt or flaky salt

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.description: In a bowl, combine discard, warm water, yeast, brown sugar, and salt. Mix briefly, then add flour and softened butter. Knead by hand 8-10 minutes or in a stand mixer with dough hook 6-7 minutes until smooth, slightly stiff, and not sticky.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, stiff pretzel dough — stiffer than bread dough. Springs back when poked.

steps.title: First Rise
steps.description: Place dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and let rise at room temp until doubled — about 60-90 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Dough doubled in size, feels airy.

steps.title: Shape Pretzels
steps.description: Divide dough into 8 equal pieces (~75g each). Roll each into a 24-inch rope, thicker in the middle and thinner toward the ends. Form a U-shape, cross the ends twice, then fold down to form the classic pretzel shape. Place on parchment-lined baking sheets.
steps.visualCue: Classic pretzel shape with two loops and three bumps at the bottom.

steps.title: Rest
steps.description: Cover shaped pretzels with a damp towel and rest 15 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Pretzels puff slightly.

steps.title: Baking Soda Bath
steps.description: Preheat oven to 450°F/232°C. Bring 2 liters of water to a boil, add baking soda carefully (it will foam). Boil pretzels 30-40 seconds per side. This is what creates the chewy crust and deep color. Remove with a slotted spoon, return to lined baking sheet.
steps.visualCue: Pretzels puff slightly in the water bath, surface becomes slightly gelatinous and matte.

steps.title: Egg Wash and Salt
steps.description: Brush each pretzel with egg wash. Sprinkle with coarse pretzel salt.
steps.visualCue: Glossy, evenly coated surface.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Bake at 450°F/232°C for 12-15 minutes until deep mahogany brown. The high heat and baking soda bath create the pretzel's characteristic dark, glossy, chewy exterior.
steps.visualCue: Deep brown (almost dark brown) crust, glossy surface, firm when tapped.

tips: The baking soda bath is non-negotiable — it's what makes a pretzel a pretzel (Maillard reaction boost).
Lye (sodium hydroxide) gives an even deeper color and authentic German pretzel flavor, but baking soda is the safe home alternative.
Shape the ropes while the dough is relaxed — if it springs back, let it rest 5 more minutes.
Best eaten the day they're made. Reheat in a 350°F/175°C oven 5 minutes to revive chewiness.

name: Sourdough Focaccia
description: A pillowy, olive-oil-drenched focaccia with a crispy bottom, airy crumb, and sea salt topping — deeply flavorful from a long cold ferment.

ingredients.item: toppings: olives, cherry tomatoes, caramelized onions, sliced garlic

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: In a large bowl, whisk together flour, water, and starter until no dry flour remains. Add salt and 2 tbsp of the olive oil and mix until combined. The dough is very wet and sticky — that's correct for focaccia.
steps.visualCue: Shaggy, very wet dough. Does not look like bread dough — more like thick batter.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation with Folds
steps.description: Over 3-4 hours at room temp, perform 4 sets of stretch-and-folds every 30-45 minutes. After each fold the dough will gradually become smoother and more cohesive despite its high hydration.
steps.visualCue: After bulk: dough is billowy, jiggly, 50-75% larger, and pulls away from bowl sides. Very airy.

steps.title: Pan and Cold Retard
steps.description: Pour 2 tbsp olive oil into a 9x13-inch rimmed baking pan. Turn dough out into the pan and gently fold it over itself once. Cover tightly with plastic wrap and refrigerate 12-16 hours (overnight). Cold retard builds flavor and makes the dough easier to handle.
steps.visualCue: Dough fills most of the pan and looks relaxed.

steps.title: Room Temp Proof
steps.description: Remove pan from fridge and let proof at room temp 2-4 hours. Gently stretch the dough to fill any corners it hasn't reached.
steps.visualCue: Dough fills the pan, puffed, jiggly, clearly airy with bubbles visible.

steps.title: Dimple and Top
steps.description: Preheat oven to 450°F/232°C. Drizzle remaining olive oil over the dough. With oiled fingers, press deep dimples all over the surface — press all the way to the pan bottom. The dimples fill with oil and create focaccia's signature appearance. Scatter rosemary, toppings, and flaky salt.
steps.visualCue: Surface is covered in deep, evenly spaced dimples filled with olive oil. Dough visibly jiggles.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Bake at 450°F/232°C for 22-28 minutes until the top is deep golden brown and the edges are crispy. The bottom should be sizzling in the olive oil. Lift a corner with a spatula to check — bottom should be golden and crispy.
steps.visualCue: Deep golden top, crispy sizzling edges and bottom. Dimples are visible and golden. Internal temp ~200°F/93°C.

steps.title: Cool Briefly
steps.description: Transfer to a cutting board after 5 minutes. Focaccia is best eaten warm or at room temp within a few hours of baking.
steps.visualCue: Airy, open crumb when torn. Crispy exterior, tender interior.

tips: Focaccia is extremely forgiving — it's one of the best first sourdough projects for beginners.
The generous olive oil is what makes focaccia focaccia — don't reduce it.
Cold-fermented focaccia (overnight in pan) has dramatically better flavor than same-day.
Press dimples just before baking, not before proofing — you want to preserve the gas.
Leftovers make incredible sandwiches.

name: Sourdough Baguettes
description: Classic French baguettes leavened entirely with sourdough starter — thin, crackly crust, creamy open crumb, and an authentic tang that commercial yeast cannot replicate.

ingredients.item: whole wheat or T65 flour (optional, for flavor)

steps.title: Autolyse
steps.description: Mix flour(s) and 340g of the cold water until no dry flour remains. Cover and autolyse 45-60 minutes. Cold water gives you more working time and better flavor.
steps.visualCue: Rough dough, completely hydrated.

steps.title: Add Starter and Salt
steps.description: Add starter, salt, and remaining 20g water. Use the pincer method to incorporate: pinch through the dough with your thumb and forefinger repeatedly, then fold over. Repeat 3-4 times until fully incorporated.
steps.visualCue: Dough is smooth and cohesive. Final dough temp should be 75-76°F/24°C.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Bulk ferment at 75°F/24°C for 3.5-4.5 hours with 4 sets of stretch-and-folds (every 30 min for the first 2 hours). Baguette bulk is slightly shorter than a country loaf — aim for 40-50% volume increase.
steps.visualCue: Dough has grown 40-50%, is smooth and extensible, bubbles on sides. Do not over-ferment or baguettes won't have oven spring.

steps.title: Divide and Pre-shape
steps.description: Turn dough gently onto an unfloured surface. Divide into 3 equal pieces (~300g each). Pre-shape each into a rough cylinder by folding the top third down and bottom third up (like a letter fold). Seam-side down. Rest uncovered.
steps.visualCue: Cylinders hold shape, no spreading.

steps.title: Final Shape
steps.description: Final-shape each baguette: place cylinder seam-side up on a lightly floured surface. Pat gently into a rectangle. Fold the top third down pressing with fingertips to seal. Fold the bottom edge up to the center pressing again. Then fold in half lengthwise and seal the seam with the heel of your hand. Roll outward from the center to ~16 inches, tapering the ends.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, taut cylinder about 16 inches long, uniform diameter.

steps.title: Proof in Couche (Cold Retard)
steps.description: Dust a linen couche heavily with flour or rice flour. Lay baguettes seam-side down in the couche with folds between each to hold shape. Cover with the overhanging couche fabric or plastic wrap. Refrigerate 8-12 hours.
steps.visualCue: Baguettes hold their shape in the couche, slightly puffed.

steps.title: Preheat with Steam Setup
steps.description: Preheat oven to 500°F/260°C with a baking steel or heavy baking sheet on the upper-middle rack and a cast-iron skillet on the floor of the oven. Allow 60 minutes to preheat fully.
steps.visualCue: Steel and oven are fully saturated with heat.

steps.title: Score and Bake with Steam
steps.description: Transfer cold baguettes from couche to a peel or inverted baking sheet dusted with semolina. Score each with 3-5 overlapping slashes at a very shallow 15-20° angle. Slide onto the hot steel. Immediately pour 1 cup of boiling water into the cast-iron skillet on the oven floor and close the door. Bake with steam 12 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Ears open dramatically, baguettes spring up. Visible rapid expansion in first 8 minutes.

steps.title: Finish Bake (No Steam)
steps.description: Remove the water pan (and crack the oven door briefly to vent steam). Reduce to 460°F/238°C and bake 12-15 more minutes until deep golden brown with some char on the ridges of the scores.
steps.visualCue: Deep golden to amber color, ears well-defined and golden-brown on both sides, crust sounds hollow when tapped.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on a wire rack 20-30 minutes. Baguettes are best eaten within 4-6 hours of baking.
steps.visualCue: Crust sings and crackles as it cools. Holes are irregular and open when cut.

tips: Shallow scoring angle (15-20°) is essential for baguette ears — straight-down scoring gives a split, not an ear.
Steam in the first 12 minutes is mandatory — without it the crust sets before the loaf can spring.
A baking steel conducts heat better than a stone, giving baguettes a crispier bottom.
Baguettes stale very quickly — freeze any extras the day they're made and reheat from frozen at 400°F/200°C for 6-8 minutes.
The biggest baguette mistake is over-fermenting during bulk — aim for 40-50% rise max.

name: Sourdough Ciabatta
description: Italian ciabatta ('slipper') bread with an extremely open, custardy crumb and thin, chewy crust — made with a sourdough biga preferment for complexity.

steps.title: Make Biga (Stiff Preferment)
steps.description: Mix 100g starter + 200g bread flour + 130g cold water into a stiff dough (65% hydration). Knead 2-3 minutes until smooth. Cover and ferment at room temp 2-3 hours until slightly puffed but not over-risen, then refrigerate 8-12 hours overnight.
steps.visualCue: Stiff, firm dough that smells pleasantly sour after the overnight rest.

steps.title: Mix Final Dough (Autolyse)
steps.description: Remove biga from fridge 30 min before use. In a stand mixer bowl (ciabatta really benefits from a mixer), combine final dough flour and 250g warm water. Mix briefly and autolyse 30 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Rough, hydrated dough.

steps.title: Add Biga, Salt, Oil
steps.description: Tear the biga into chunks and add to the autolysed dough along with salt, olive oil, and remaining 30g water. Mix with a dough hook on medium 8-12 minutes until the dough passes the windowpane test. Ciabatta dough is wet (~80%) — it will slap against the mixer bowl walls and slowly come together.
steps.visualCue: Dough clears the bowl sides, becomes smooth and very elastic, stretches thin without tearing in the windowpane test. Still quite slack.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation with Coil Folds
steps.description: Transfer dough to a lightly oiled container. Bulk ferment 3-4 hours at 78°F/26°C. Perform coil folds every 30 minutes for the first 2 hours (4 sets). The dough must be strong enough to hold its airy structure.
steps.visualCue: Dough is very airy and wobbly, with large bubbles visible. Grown 60-80%.

steps.title: Divide and Shape (Minimal Handling)
steps.description: CRITICAL: Handle this dough as little as possible to preserve the open structure. Generously flour a work surface and your hands. Gently scrape the dough out. Divide into two pieces with a bench scraper — do NOT round or pre-shape. Each piece should look like a rectangle. Gently coax each into a rough rectangular slipper shape and transfer (using the bench scraper) seam-side down onto a generously floured couche or heavily floured parchment.
steps.visualCue: Rough, bubbly rectangles. You can see large bubbles through the dough. Do not degas.

steps.title: Proof
steps.description: Cover and proof at room temp 45-60 minutes, or refrigerate 4-8 hours for more flavor.
steps.visualCue: Loaves puff noticeably. Bubbles still visible.

steps.title: Bake with Steam
steps.description: Preheat oven to 500°F/260°C with a baking steel and steam setup (as in baguette recipe). Quickly flip each ciabatta from parchment/couche onto the hot steel — do not score ciabatta. Pour boiling water for steam. Bake 12 min with steam, then vent steam, reduce to 460°F/238°C, and bake 12-15 more minutes until golden with a thin, crispy crust.
steps.visualCue: Thin, blistered golden crust, dramatic oven spring. Holes clearly visible when cut — wild, irregular, lacy crumb.

steps.title: Cool
steps.description: Cool on wire rack 30-45 minutes. The interior crumb structure continues to set.
steps.visualCue: Extremely open, webby crumb with large and small irregular holes.

tips: A stand mixer is practically required — hand-kneading this wet a dough to full development is very difficult.
Do not shape ciabatta like a normal loaf — minimal handling preserves the open crumb.
Ciabatta does not get scored — the open crumb develops naturally with steam.
Ciabatta is incredible for sandwiches due to its chewy, open crumb.

name: Sourdough Dinner Rolls
description: Pillowy, soft dinner rolls with a feathery crumb and a gentle sourdough tang — perfect for holiday tables or everyday meals.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: In a stand mixer bowl, combine flour, warm milk, starter, eggs, sugar, and salt. Mix with dough hook on low 2 minutes. Increase to medium and knead 6 minutes. Add softened butter 1 tablespoon at a time, allowing each addition to fully incorporate before adding the next. Continue kneading 6-8 more minutes until dough is smooth, silky, and passes the windowpane test.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, satiny dough that clears the bowl sides. Stretches to paper-thin transparency in the windowpane test without tearing.

steps.title: Bulk Fermentation
steps.description: Place dough in a lightly buttered bowl. Cover and bulk ferment at 75-78°F/24-26°C for 4-5 hours until doubled. Perform 2-3 sets of stretch-and-folds in the first 1.5 hours.
steps.visualCue: Dough doubled, pillowy, pulls away from bowl sides cleanly.

steps.title: Divide and Shape Rolls
steps.description: Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface. Divide into 12 equal pieces (~75g each). Shape each into a tight ball by pulling the edges down and under, rotating on the bench to build tension. Arrange in a greased 9x13-inch baking dish, spaced evenly.
steps.visualCue: 12 smooth, taut balls, evenly sized, touching slightly in the pan.

steps.title: Proof (Cold Retard Overnight)
steps.description: Cover the pan with plastic wrap and refrigerate 8-12 hours overnight. Remove from fridge 1.5-2 hours before baking to allow rolls to come to room temp and complete their proof.
steps.visualCue: After room temp rise: rolls are puffy, touching each other, and jiggle slightly when you shake the pan.

steps.title: Bake
steps.description: Preheat oven to 375°F/190°C. Bake rolls 22-26 minutes until golden brown on top. Internal temp should reach 190°F/88°C.
steps.visualCue: Deep golden tops, baked through to the center. Sides pale where rolls were touching.

steps.title: Brush with Butter
steps.description: Immediately brush with melted butter straight out of the oven for a glossy, soft crust.
steps.visualCue: Glossy, golden, irresistibly soft rolls.

tips: Adding butter slowly (after initial kneading) is key to enriched dough — adding it all at once inhibits gluten development.
If dough feels greasy, refrigerate 20 min and continue kneading — butter firms up.
The rolls should be touching in the pan — this gives the soft, pull-apart sides.
Make 24 slider rolls instead by dividing into 24 pieces (~38g each).

name: Sourdough English Muffins
description: Nook-and-cranny-filled sourdough English muffins cooked on a griddle — far superior to store-bought, with a satisfying tang and chewy texture.

steps.title: Mix Dough (Night Before)
steps.description: Combine flour, milk, water, starter, honey, and salt. Knead by hand or in a mixer 6-8 minutes until smooth. Add softened butter and knead 4-5 more minutes. Dough should be slightly tacky and smooth. Cover and refrigerate overnight.
steps.visualCue: Smooth, slightly tacky dough. Supple and not too stiff.

steps.title: Add Baking Soda and Rest
steps.description: Remove dough from fridge. Flatten on a lightly floured surface, sprinkle 1/4 tsp baking soda over the surface, fold in, and knead briefly 2-3 minutes to incorporate. Rest 30 minutes.
steps.visualCue: Dough is slightly more airy and active after soda is incorporated.

steps.title: Roll and Cut
steps.description: Roll dough on a cornmeal-dusted surface to 1/2-inch thickness. Use a 3-4 inch round cutter or an empty can to cut muffins. Gather scraps, re-roll, and cut again. Place cut muffins on a parchment sheet dusted with cornmeal. Dust tops with more cornmeal.
steps.visualCue: Round discs about 1/2-inch thick, coated in cornmeal.

steps.title: Proof
steps.description: Cover cut muffins with a kitchen towel and proof at room temp 45-60 minutes until slightly puffy.
steps.visualCue: Muffins puff slightly, about 3/4-inch tall.

steps.title: Griddle Cook
steps.description: Heat a cast-iron griddle or large skillet over LOW heat (this is important — low and slow). Lightly grease with butter. Cook muffins 6-8 minutes per side. Low heat allows the interior to cook through without burning the exterior. Resist pressing them down.
steps.visualCue: Even golden-brown surface on both sides. Muffin rises slightly on the griddle. When tapped, sides feel firm (not doughy).

steps.title: Cool and Fork-Split
steps.description: Cool on a wire rack 15 minutes. ALWAYS fork-split English muffins — use a fork all the way around the equator, then pull apart. This preserves the interior nooks and crannies that make them special.
steps.visualCue: Irregular, cratered interior full of nooks and crannies when fork-split. Toast in a toaster for 2-3 minutes until golden.

tips: Low, slow griddle heat is essential — rushing with high heat burns the outside and leaves a doughy interior.
Always fork-split, never knife-cut — the nooks and crannies are what hold butter and jam.
The baking soda addition creates the characteristic rise and nook structure.
English muffins freeze beautifully — freeze in a zip bag and toast from frozen.

name: Sourdough Discard Flour Tortillas
description: Soft, pliable flour tortillas with a gentle tang from sourdough discard — cook in minutes in a dry skillet and taste far better than store-bought.

steps.title: Mix Dough
steps.description: In a bowl, whisk flour, salt, and baking powder. Add the fat and work it into the flour with your fingers until the mixture resembles coarse sand (like making pie crust). Add discard and warm water and mix until a soft, pliable dough forms. Knead in the bowl 2-3 minutes until smooth.
steps.visualCue: Soft, smooth, non-sticky dough. Like modeling clay consistency.

steps.title: Rest
steps.description: Wrap dough in plastic wrap or cover with an inverted bowl and rest at room temp 30-60 minutes. This relaxes the gluten so tortillas roll out easily without springing back.
steps.visualCue: Dough is noticeably more relaxed and soft after resting.

steps.title: Divide and Roll
steps.description: Divide dough into 8 equal balls (~60g each). Keep covered while working. On a lightly floured surface, roll each ball into a thin 8-inch round. Roll from center outward, rotating 90° between rolls. The thinner, the better — you should almost see through it.
steps.visualCue: Nearly translucent in the thinnest spots. Edges may be slightly uneven — that's rustic and fine.

steps.title: Cook in Dry Skillet
steps.description: Heat a cast-iron skillet or comal over medium-high heat until very hot (no oil needed). Cook each tortilla 45-60 seconds per side until light brown spots form and the tortilla puffs slightly.
steps.visualCue: Irregular golden-brown spots on both sides. Tortilla puffs in one or more spots — sign of proper heat.

steps.title: Keep Warm
steps.description: Stack cooked tortillas in a tortilla warmer or wrap in a clean kitchen towel to keep warm and pliable. Serve immediately.
steps.visualCue: Pliable, soft tortillas that fold without cracking.

tips: Lard makes the most tender, authentic-tasting tortillas; neutral oil works well as a substitute.
The rest period is not optional — without it, tortillas spring back as you roll and tear during cooking.
Cook in a dry, very hot pan — oil makes them greasy.
Tortillas stiffen as they cool; reheat in a dry pan 20-30 seconds to restore pliability.
Store in a zip bag at room temp for 2-3 days, or refrigerate up to 1 week.
